    pioneeR User Manual

    Ove Haugland Jakobsen, Jan Roar Beckstrøm

    2019-04-24

    user-guide.Rmd

    Introduction

    pioneeR is a webapp for Data Envelopment Analysis (DEA), programmed in R and based on the R Shiny framework for the user interface.

    This app uses the R packages Benchmarking, rDEA and productivity to estimate efficiency scores using data envelopment analysis.

    This guide describes rather briefly the main functionality of pioneeR. The app should be easy to use, but please remember: Ease of use is no substitute for knowledge about DEA. Thus, we strongly recommend users to familiarize themselves with the theory and principles behind DEA when using the app.

    Running the app

    To run the app, enter the following code in the R console. The app should open in your browser:

    pioneeR::runPioneeR()

    You can optionally sent data that is loaded into your global environment to the app when it launches. Use the x argument in the runPioneeR() call to specify a dataset you want to analyse. This can be handy if you already have data loaded into your R session, or if you have data in a format that is not supported by the upload function in the app (see below for supported formats).

    Upload file for analysis

    Start estimating efficiency scores by uploading a dataset (press Browse). The app supports the following formats:

    • Excel
    • Stata
    • Comma separated files (CSV)
    • Semi-colon separated files (CSV)

    For Excel and CSV files, the first row should contain the variables names. Estimating efficiency scores require that all input and output variables are numeric. The app will try to force all string variables to numeric when a data file is submitted.

    The first variable, or column, in the dataset is assumed to be the ID variable for the decision making units (DMUs) in the analysis. If values in the ID variable are not unique, the app will assume that the data is time series data. You can override the ID variable after the file is uploaded.

    Selecting variables and DMUs

    After a file has been uploaded and verified, you can select your input and output variables in the Data tab. The app will show a preview of the dataset with your selected variables. Note that all input and output variables must be numeric. By default, all observations in the dataset are included in the analysis. You can subset the data by clicking on the row of the observation you wish to include. Selected DMUs will be highlighted in the table. If you want to include all but a few observations, press Select all to include all DMUs and then click in the corresponding rows for the observations you wish to exclude. Note that you need to select Subset data in order to perform the subsetting.

    If you have time series data, and want to perform an analysis of Malmquist index, you can press Time series data and select the variable that includes data on each period. For more information, see below.

    Bookmarking

    The Data tab also includes a Bookmark state functionality. Pressing this button saves the “state” of the app and hence also the state of the data and the analysis and provides a window called “Bookmarked application link” with an URL. You use this URL to restore the app.

    Bookmark state is useful if you want to save the analysis in the app to continue working with it at a later stage. When you bookmark the analysis, a copy of the data and the parameters used in the analysis are stored on the server. Note that bookmarked analyses might be purged from the server from time to time.

    Note that if you are running the app locally, the Bookmarking function may not work as expected. This is because the URL to the bookmarked state will include the port number used in the Shiny session. You can specify the port number when you run the app, by setting the port parameter in the runPioneeR() call. Note that bookmarked sessions will be saved to the pioneeR directory in your R library. Bookmarked sessions will be deleted when you update or reinstall pioneeR. If you are unsure where your library is located, you can run .libPaths() from the R console.

    Estimating productivity scores

    Press Analyse to start estimating efficiency scores (DEA analysis). If necessary, you can go back to the data tab to change input or output variables.

    Selecting returns to scale

    You can select among the following types of returns to scale:

    • Variable (VRS)
    • Constant (CRS)
    • Non-increasing (NIRS)
    • Non-descreasing (NDRS)

    For more information on the differences between these options, please consult a textbook in Data Envelopment Analysis.

    Orientation

    The results estimated depends on the orientation of the data model. The results can either be input- or output oriented.

    Normalize input/output

    When the inputs and outputs have different scales, it can be necessary to normalize the input and output variables. Normalizing data is done in two steps. First the column mean is calculated for each column in the input and output matrices respectively:

    \[\bar{V}_i=\frac{\sum_{n-1}^N V_{ni}}{N}\]

    Where \(\bar{V}_i\) is the mean value for column \(i\) (an input or output), \(N\) is the number of DMUs and \(V_{ni}\) is the value of DMU \(n\) for a given input or output \(i\).

    \[Vnorm_{ni}=\frac{V_{ni}}{V_i}\]

    Where \(Vnorm_{ni}\) is the normalized value for the value associated with DMU \(n\) and input or output in column \(i\).

    Normalizing data will not affect the estimated efficiency scores, but will give different estimates for slack.

    Output options

    The output options provides the following options:

    • Show slack: Slack can be defined as the additional improvement (increase in outputs and/or decrease in inputs) needed for a DMU to become efficient.
    • Show super efficiency score: Super efficiency is a method for ranking efficient DMUs. Note that super efficiency is presented as sDEA in the results matrix.

    Export results

    You can export the data with the estimated efficiency scores for each DMU. You can choose between Excel, CSV and Stata as the output format. For the time being it is only possible to export results from the Efficiency-tab. Results relating to for example the Peers-tab or the Scale efficiency-tab are not available for export. This may be included in future versions.

    Generate analysis report

    The Generate analysis report functionality creates a print quality PDF-report containing all analysis results and plots, and the code used to generate it. This is highly useful for documenting the analysis, including replication of the analysis and reproduction of results.

    Download data for analysis

    When loading data into pioneeR the allowed file types are Excel, Stata and CSV. When data is imported, it is converted to a standard data frame in R. You can export the data in R format for further analysis using R, or for documenting the analysis (see Generate analysis report above). Click Download data for analysis to download the data as a RDS file. In R, you can load the file with the readRDS() function. The data file can be used in conjunction with the code supplied in the analysis report.

    The Results Matrix

    The results matrix provides the following tabs:

    • Efficiency
    • Slack
    • Plot
    • Peers
    • Summary
    • Scale efficiency

    The default tab is the Efficiency tab where you will as default only get a list of the DMUs and their individual efficiency scores, based on your selection of variables, returns to scale, orientation etc.

    You can sort results by decision-making units, efficiency scores, slack or super efficiency scores.

    Efficiency

    The app presents efficiency scores with five decimals to ensure high accuracy. To change this, specify your preferred level of accuracy in the Number of decimals box. The app allows you to sort your results.

    Slack

    You can have either input or output slack, depending on whether your model is input- or output oriented. Thus, if you change model orientation, the slack score will automatically change accordingly.

    Plot

    Under the plot tab you get two different plots.

    The first is a simple distribution plot of input and output, with a line showing constant return to scale.1

    The second is a so-called salter diagram with bars showing the efficiency score for each DMU, and where the width of the bar shows the size of combined input for each DMU.

    Peers

    Peers, or peer group, is the (small) group of efficient units “closest” to the different DMUs. “Close” in this context means similar in terms of the input/output mix. The number of peers is restricted to maximum three peers, where “peer1” is the most similar.

    Summary

    Under summary you get, well, a summary of the DEA analysis, including returns to scale, orientation, quartile scores, and mean- and weighted efficiency.

    Weighted efficiency is computed by taking into account the combined inputs of each DMU. The weighted efficiency gives an estimate of the potential efficiency gains for the population.

    You also get a table with the number of DMUs (observations) categorised on an efficiency range and a corresponding bar plot showing the distribution of efficiency scores.

    Scale efficiency

    Scale efficiency is based on the simple notion that while DMUs can be efficient given input/output mix, there is the possibility that units could be even more efficient on a different scale of production. Thus, a unit can potentially be efficient in a VRS model, but inefficient under a CRS assumption.

    Scale efficiency, noted scale_eff in the app, is simply calculated as the ratio between the CRS and VRS scores.

    The ration between VRS and NIRS is also calculated and given as vrs_nirs

    Malmquist indices

    When you work with time series data you will not get the Analyse tab but get a tab called Malmquist instead.

    With the app, you can estimate productivity changes over time using the Malmquist Index. By pressing Time series data the app will estimate technological changes, efficiency changes and productivity changes. This requires that you have a variable that identifies the unique time periods in your data. The time period is usually a year, but it can also be another time unit. If a file has duplicates in the ID variable (the variable that identifies unique DMUs), the app will assume that the data is time-based. The app will then automatically check for variables that can be converted to integers and include sensible values for years (minimum value greater than 1900, and maximum value less than 2100) and set this as the time variable.

    There is only one results tab under Malmquist showing the change from a reference year to the next, with the Malmquist score and the corresponding scores for change in efficiency and technology.

    Important: Data used for creating Malmquist indices must be balanced. This means that all DMUs must have data for all years in the analysis. The app will automatically check if the data is balanced before performing a time series analysis. If the data is not balanced, listwise deletion will be performed. Only observations with complete information will be kept. For example, if you have 60 DMUs in your file, but for a single year, you only have observations for 50 DMUs, this year will be excluded from the analysis. Note that this process of listwise deletion might introduce gap years in the analysis.

    1. Even if you have only one unit on the CRS-line, there may be more units with a efficiency score of 1 (100 % efficient). This is due to the fact that there may be units which are so different from all other that they are incomparable to all other DMUs. Such units will normally be defined as 100 % effective, but they don’t necessarily lie on the CRS-line.↩

    Contents

    • Introduction
    • Running the app
    • Upload file for analysis
    • Selecting variables and DMUs
    • Estimating productivity scores
    • Malmquist indices

    Developed by Ove Haugland Jakobsen.
